name: Raghunatha Shiromani
dates_notes: Navadvīpa Naiyāyika; dates fixed by Bhattacharya (1958, History of Navya-Nyāya in Mithilā) and Matilal (1968) to the late fifteenth and early sixteenth century. Caitanya is a near-contemporary Navadvīpa figure; the two are traditionally placed in the same milieu without secure interaction.

lineage_polemical.note: Yaśovijaya engages the Padārtha-Tattva-Nirūpaṇa's reduction of the Vaiśeṣika categories from seven to three; the Jaina-Tarka-Bhāṣā discusses the reduction in light of the Jaina dravya-paryāya analysis, and the methodological convergence on category-reduction is itself a significant cross-tradition moment.

core_thesis: Raghunātha Śiromaṇi is the figure in whom Navya-Nyāya passes from Mithilā to Navadvīpa and acquires its mature scholastic form. Two contributions are systemic. First, the *Tattva-Cintāmaṇi-Dīdhiti* sub-commentates Gaṅgeśa with a precision and concision that becomes the standard Navadvīpa reading: every Gaṅgeśa definition is re-examined, the *Vyāpti-Pañcaka* analysis is refined further (in *Dīdhiti* on *Tattva-Cintāmaṇi*, *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa*, *vyāpti-pañcaka-prakaraṇa*), and the *avacchedaka*-machinery is tightened to handle cases Gaṅgeśa had left to commentarial discretion. The *Dīdhiti* is the form in which the *Cintāmaṇi* is read in the Navadvīpa pāṭhaśālās and in which it propagates into Bengal Vaiṣṇava and Mithilā Mādhva scholasticism. Second, the *Padārtha-Tattva-Nirūpaṇa* — an independent treatise on Vaiśeṣika ontology — reduces the canonical seven *padārthas* (*dravya*, *guṇa*, *karma*, *sāmānya*, *viśeṣa*, *samavāya*, *abhāva*; cf. Praśastapāda's *Padārtha-Dharma-Saṅgraha*) to three. Raghunātha argues (*Padārtha-Tattva-Nirūpaṇa*, *sāmānya-* and *viśeṣa-nirūpaṇa* sections) that *sāmānya* (universal) and *viśeṣa* (individuator) collapse into *svarūpa* (intrinsic nature) once Navya-Nyāya analysis of *avacchedaka* is taken seriously, and (in the *samavāya-nirūpaṇa* section) that *samavāya* (inherence) is a single-relation simplifying-postulate that ordinary cognitive practice does not require. The reduction is the most radical Navya-Nyāya intervention into Vaiśeṣika ontology and is the precedent the school continues to debate. The *Dīdhiti* attracts the major Navadvīpa sub-commentary stack — Mathurānātha's *Māthurī*, Jagadīśa's *Jāgadīśī*, Gadādhara's *Gādādharī* — and is the form in which Navya-Nyāya propagates into the Sanskrit dialectical mainstream of the late-medieval and early-modern period.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Cintamani-Didhiti
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition placed in Raghunātha's mature Navadvīpa period.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Gaṅgeśa's Tattva-Cintāmaṇi; the standard Navadvīpa reading.
engaged_works.summary: Sub-commentary on Gaṅgeśa's *Tattva-Cintāmaṇi*, organised by *khaṇḍa* (*Pratyakṣa-*, *Anumāna-*, *Upamāna-*, *Śabda-*) and proceeding gloss-by-gloss through Gaṅgeśa's prose. The *Dīdhiti*'s technical achievement is to refine the *avacchedaka*-machinery (in *Dīdhiti* on *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa*, *avacchedakatva-prakaraṇa*) to a precision that allows every cognition, every inference, and every property-instantiation to be specified without remainder. Raghunātha tightens Gaṅgeśa's *vyāpti*-definitions further (in *Dīdhiti* on the *vyāpti-pañcaka-prakaraṇa*), identifies cases where the *Cintāmaṇi*'s formulations admit unintended satisfiers, and supplies the corrected formulations the school inherits. The *Anumāna-Khaṇḍa-Dīdhiti* is the most studied portion; its analysis of *paryāpti* (in the *paryāpti-vāda* gloss), *anyathāsiddhi*, and *upādhi*-defeaters becomes the standard reference for the next two centuries. The *Dīdhiti* attracts a major sub-commentary stack: Mathurānātha's *Māthurī*, Jagadīśa's *Jāgadīśī*, and Gadādhara's *Gādādharī* all take it as base. The work is the conduit through which Navya-Nyāya passes into late-medieval Bengali Vaiṣṇava theology (Jīva Gosvāmī's *Sandarbhas*, Baladeva's *Govinda-Bhāṣya* on Brahma-Sūtra 1.1.1) and into the Mādhva *Tarka-Tāṇḍava*-stream of Vyāsatīrtha's epigones.

engaged_works.title: Padartha-Tattva-Nirupana
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition placed in Raghunātha's Navadvīpa period.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored independent treatise on Vaiśeṣika ontology.
engaged_works.summary: Compact independent treatise reformulating Vaiśeṣika categorial ontology. The canonical seven *padārthas* — *dravya* (substance), *guṇa* (quality), *karma* (motion), *sāmānya* (universal), *viśeṣa* (individuator), *samavāya* (inherence), *abhāva* (absence), as systematized in Praśastapāda's *Padārtha-Dharma-Saṅgraha* — are reduced to three: *dravya*, *guṇa*, *karma*. The argumentative spine is methodological. *Sāmānya* and *viśeṣa* are argued to be redundant (in the *sāmānya-* and *viśeṣa-nirūpaṇa* sections of the *Padārtha-Tattva-Nirūpaṇa*) once *svarūpa-sambandha* (the relation a thing bears to itself in virtue of its intrinsic nature) is accepted as primitive: the work the universal does in fixing what counts as a similar instance and the work the individuator does in distinguishing two paramāṇus of the same kind (cf. Vaiśeṣika-Sūtra 1.2.3–6 and 7.1.8–11) are both performed, on Raghunātha's analysis, by *svarūpa* alone. *Samavāya* (in the *samavāya-nirūpaṇa* section) — the inherence-relation that Vaiśeṣika posits to relate quality to substance, motion to substance, universal to individual — is argued to be a needless single-relation postulate: each ostensible inherence-case can be analysed as the substantive's bearing the relevant property in virtue of its *svarūpa*. *Abhāva* is retained but reanalysed via *pratiyogitā* in fully Navya-Nyāya form (in the *abhāva-nirūpaṇa* section). The treatise is the school's most radical ontological intervention and remains debated in late-Navadvīpa scholasticism.
